data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T01:45:25.535Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about EF Core and the Cosmos DB provider. Let me answer based on what I know.

In EF Core, the Azure Cosmos DB provider has been moving toward async-only APIs. The key breaking change is:

In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated but still available via an opt-in mechanism (`EnableSyncOverAsync` or similar configuration). The Cosmos DB provider doesn't natively support synchronous operations because the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only. So EF Core was doing "sync-over-async" internally (blocking on async calls).


In EF Core 11 (.NET 11), that sync-over-async support would likely be completely removed for the Cosmos DB provider. I should note that my training data has a cutoff, so I'm not certain about the exact timeline or specific changes in .NET 11 since those versions are in the future relative to my knowledge.

What I do know is that EF Core 9 already introduced a breaking change where synchronous I/O was disabled by default for Cosmos DB, though you could opt back in with `EnableSyncOverAsync()`. The trend has been moving away from sync support entirely, so the logical next step would be removing that opt-in completely, forcing developers to use async operations or face exceptions with no workaround. The breaking change removes the opt-in configuration entirely, so all synchronous operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` will throw `InvalidOperationException` with no way to re-enable them. The fix is converting to async equivalents...
